0: <title>Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) of Industrial Milk Production </title>
TINE Norwegian Dairies, Centre for Research and Developement, P.O. Box 7 Kalbakken, N-0902 OSLO, Norway; merete.hogaas.eide@tine.no
1: Abstract. A Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) was carried out for milk production extending from the origin of the inputs to the agricultural step to the consumer phase and the waste manage- ment of the packaging. Three Norwegian dairies of different sizes and degree of automation were studied. The main objec- tives were to find any hot spots in the life cycle of milk, to deter- mine the significance of the dairy size and degree of automa- tion, and to study the influence of transport. The agriculture was found to be the main hot spot for almost all the environ- mental themes studied, although the dairy processing, packag- ing, consumer phase and waste management were also of im- portance. The consumer phase was the main contributor to photo-oxidant formation and important regarding eutrophica- tion. The small dairy was found to have a greater environmen- tal impact than the middle-sized and the largest dairies. The transport did not have any major influence.

Keywords: Consumer; dairy; dairy processing; detergents; dis- tribution; industrial milk production; LCA; life cycle assessment (LCA); milk; milk farming; milk packaging; milk production; packaging; transport; waste management
2: The production of milk requires large inputs of resources, while it also causes several negative environmental effects. Life cycle assessment (LCA) has been used extensively to study milk packaging (Oterholm et al. 1994, Tillman et al. 1991, Mekel et al. 1990, Baumann et al. 1993, Ponsford 1993), as well as milk farming (Cederberg 1998, Cederberg and Mattsson 2000, Hogaas and Moller 1997, Meeusen- van Onna and Leneman 1996). Hogaas Eide (1998), Rydberg et al. (1995) and Moller and Hogaas (1997) have concen- trated on dairy processing. However, the whole life cycle of milk including measured site-specific data from dairies and their transports in order to determine the influence of dairy size and automation, as well as the influence of their trans- ports, has not been addressed.

For many food products, for example meat (Moller and Vold 1995), ham and bread (Weidema et al. 1995) and bread (Andersson 1998), the agricultural steps of food production contribute more to the environmental impact than the con- sumer phase, the processing or the transport; although, when Andersson (1998) investigated the production of tomato ketchup, she found that packaging and food processing were hot spots for several impact categories. For carrot purrée,

studied by Mattsson (1999), the food processing accounted for approximately half of the total energy use. The food processing was also found to be a hot spot for cereal-based baby food (Mattsson 1999).

The environmental impacts of drinking milk were investi- gated in this study, from the production of inputs (to agri- cultural production and fodder) to the milk consumption, as well as the waste management of the packaging. How- ever, most interest was placed on the dairy processing and the dairy transport. The study was based on conventional farming. Ecological milk, which has a small share of the market (0.7% in 1999) in Norway, is still growing. The LCA methodology used is based on Lindfors et al. (1995) and ISO (1998).

4: The objectives of this study are: (1) to examine the total life cycle of milk, and to identify the parameters with the largest environmental impacts, the hot spots, of the life cycle; (2) to compare the environmental impacts due to the size and de- gree of automation of a dairy; and (3) to determine the im- portance of transport distances of the three dairies.
5: <header>1.1 Functional unit and system boundaries </header>

The functional unit was set to 1,000 litres of drinking milk brought to the consumers. In Norway there are four types of drinking milk, which differ only in the amount of fat: skimmed milk (0.1% fat), extra light milk (0.7% fat), light milk (1.5% fat) and milk (3.9% fat). The average specific weight of these types of drinking milk, based on the volume of production, is 1.033 kg per litre, which means the func- tional unit is 1,033 kg. The steps of the life cycle of milk production and consumption included in this study are shown in a flow chart (Fig. 1).

Data of milk farming. The information on farming was gen- erally Norwegian average data for conventional farming (NILF 1995, Moller and Hogaas 1997). Silage, hay and pas- ture were cultivated at the farm. Ten percent of the fodder used was concentrated feed, composed of grain, rape meal, molasses and fish meal. Data for the cultivation of rape, grain and sugar beets were taken from Cederberg (1998). The amount of pesticides used was based on Norwegian averages and data collected by Cederberg (1998). Mostly literature data was used for the agriculture, while dairy pro- cessing was of a main interest. It is, however, not correct to

exclude the agriculture; several studies have shown that the agricultural food production is the main hot spot. Informa- tion on amount and risk class of active ingredients in the dif- ferent pesticides is from PK-produkter (1997). The human toxicity of the pesticides is qualitatively assessed based on risk- phrases (R-phrases) and the Norwegian risk classification sys- tem of pesticides: (1) Risk class X: the pesticide is classified as very poisonous; (2) Risk class A: the pesticide is classified as poisonous; (3) Risk class B: the pesticide is classified as harm- ful; (4) Risk class C: the pesticide is classified as less harmful. System boundaries of agriculture have been extensively dis- cussed in Audsley et al. (1997). The following boundaries were used for the agricultural steps in this study:

<list>Interaction between crops in crop rotation is not fully taken into account 
Capital goods (machinery, buildings) were excluded from the study</list>
Land use and soil quality changes caused by cultivation were not included

The amounts of medication and pesticides leaving the sys- tem were accounted for, but not their quantitative environ- mental effects. When medication is used, e.g. penicillin, milk may not be delivered to the dairy for 8 days. This milk was assumed, as a worst case, to leave the system as wastewater, which was taken into account. Buildings and machinery were excluded, for both agriculture and the dairy industry. For the cleaning detergents, the up-stream emissions and use of resources (0.088% of the functional unit) at the farm were excluded; nevertheless, emissions of nitrogen and phospho- rus from use at the farm were included. At the farm, the milking, storage of milk, and cleaning of equipment were included, as well as the emissions of cooling medium, such as HCFC-22, from the storage tank.

Data of three dairies. The data for the dairy, the transport to the dairy, and the transport to the retail outlet were site specific. Measurements of energy and emissions were made at three dairies, representing a broad range of Norwegian dairies. The first was a small dairy (7 mill. litres a year) located in a sparsely populated area, in northern Norway, with long distances to both milk farmers (average 96 km) and retailers (average 123 km). The processing of the raw milk entering the dairy was highly automated, although the processing of the products was less so. Fresh drinking milk, cultured milk, cream and butter were produced. The second dairy was a middle-sized one (25 mill. litres a year), located in a small town in eastern Norway with average distances of 60 km to the farmers and 30 km to the retailers. This dairy was less automated than the small one. Fresh drinking milk, cultured milk and cream were produced. The third dairy, large by Norwegian standards (50 mill. litres a year), is located near a city in southwestern Norway, with short distances to milk farmers (average 49 km by lorry and 6 km by ferry) and retailers (average 18 km by lorry and 2.5 km by ferry). The dairy was highly automated. Fresh drinking milk, cultured milk, cream and yoghurt were produced. In Norway, both the farms and the dairies are smaller than in the rest of Europe. Moreover, the distances of transport are rather long. The effect of variation in transport distance was studied. Production and transport of detergents (used in dairies) and packaging were also included. Nitrogen emit- ted from use of detergents was accounted for.

The dairy registered the total consumption of diesel and the total amount of kilometres of the different lorries in addi- tion to the amount of milk transported. Then the energy use (MJ) per litre of milk could be calculated, which was used in the calculations. By use of the average distances provided above, the variation of the consumption of diesel per km at the three dairies should not be taken into account.

Data of the consumer phase. The consumer is assumed to drive 10 km for shopping, to buy 20 kg of food, and the car is assumed to consume 0.1 litres of gasoline per km. Fur- thermore, 2 kg (10%) is assumed to be milk. The amount of milk leaving with wastewater at the consumers' residence was determined by measuring the amount of COD (Chemi- cal Oxygen Demand) remaining in the carton and glasses.

The milk was assumed to be used as drinking milk and filled into glasses of 250 ml for consumption. Measured in this way, the loss of milk was 0.97%. Emissions of cooling me- dia from refrigerators in households were not included. Re- frigerators with ozone depleting media are not allowed for production and old refrigerators are taken care of in the waste management system.

Waste management. It is a Norwegian goal to recycle 60% of the milk packaging to paper products. Although the cur- rent percentage is 40, the goal of 60 percent was used in this study. Since this percentage is expected to be reached in the near future, it should reflect the Norwegian situation in a few years. The packaging was transported from the entire country to the paper mill; the recycling and the specific trans- ports were included. Of the remaining packaging, together with the other waste from dairies, 35% was assumed to be landfilled, while 65% was assumed to be incinerated with energy recovery. Emissions from incineration and landfilling were included in the study. However, CO2 emissions from renewable materials, such as paper, are not included.

The electricity model used was 90% hydropower and 10% European average electricity, based mainly on fossil fuels. The situation in Norway has changed during the past few years, from about 100% hydropower to a 5-10% import of electricity. It is assumed that the share of imported electric-

ity will be about 10% in the future (Ruud et al. 1998). Elec- tricity or natural gas was used for thermal energy produc- tion at the dairies. Table 1 shows the various steps of the life cycle of milk and its data sources.

The consumption of energy and water resources was con- sidered. The environmental parameters examined were glo- bal warming potential, acidification, eutrophication, photo- oxidant formation, depletion of stratospheric ozone and ecotoxicity. The amount of pesticides used was listed and the amount of antibiotics used was accounted for.

At the farm, the milking, storage of milk, and cleaning of equipment were included, as well as the emissions of cooling medium, such as HCFC-22, from the storage tank.

Data of three dairies. The data for the dairy, the transport to the dairy, and the transport to the retail outlet were site specific. Measurements of energy and emissions were made at three dairies, representing a broad range of Norwegian dairies. The first was a small dairy (7 mill. litres a year) located in a sparsely populated area, in northern Norway, with long distances to both milk farmers (average 96 km) and retailers (average 123 km). The processing of the raw milk entering the dairy was highly automated, although the processing of the products was less so. Fresh drinking milk, cultured milk, cream and butter were produced. The second dairy was a middle-sized one (25 mill. litres a year), located in a small town in eastern Norway with average distances of 60 km to the farmers and 30 km to the retailers. This dairy was less automated than the small one. Fresh drinking milk, cultured milk and cream were produced. The third dairy, large by Norwegian standards (50 mill. litres a year), is located near a city in southwestern Norway, with short distances to milk farmers (average 49 km by lorry and 6 km by ferry) and retailers (average 18 km by lorry and 2.5 km by ferry). The dairy was highly automated. Fresh drinking milk, cultured milk, cream and yoghurt were produced. In Norway, both the farms and the dairies are smaller than in the rest of Europe. Moreover, the distances of transport are rather long. The effect of variation in transport distance was studied. Production and transport of detergents (used in dairies) and packaging were also included. Nitrogen emit- ted from use of detergents was accounted for.

The dairy registered the total consumption of diesel and the total amount of kilometres of the different lorries in addi- tion to the amount of milk transported. Then the energy use (MJ) per litre of milk could be calculated, which was used in the calculations. By use of the average distances provided above, the variation of the consumption of diesel per km at the three dairies should not be taken into account.

Data of the consumer phase. The consumer is assumed to drive 10 km for shopping, to buy 20 kg of food, and the car is assumed to consume 0.1 litres of gasoline per km. Fur- thermore, 2 kg (10%) is assumed to be milk. The amount of milk leaving with wastewater at the consumers' residence was determined by measuring the amount of COD (Chemi- cal Oxygen Demand) remaining in the carton and glasses.

The milk was assumed to be used as drinking milk and filled into glasses of 250 ml for consumption. Measured in this way, the loss of milk was 0.97%. Emissions of cooling me- dia from refrigerators in households were not included. Re- frigerators with ozone depleting media are not allowed for production and old refrigerators are taken care of in the waste management system.

Waste management. It is a Norwegian goal to recycle 60% of the milk packaging to paper products. Although the cur- rent percentage is 40, the goal of 60 percent was used in this study. Since this percentage is expected to be reached in the near future, it should reflect the Norwegian situation in a few years. The packaging was transported from the entire country to the paper mill; the recycling and the specific trans- ports were included. Of the remaining packaging, together with the other waste from dairies, 35% was assumed to be landfilled, while 65% was assumed to be incinerated with energy recovery. Emissions from incineration and landfilling were included in the study. However, CO2 emissions from renewable materials, such as paper, are not included.

The electricity model used was 90% hydropower and 10% European average electricity, based mainly on fossil fuels. The situation in Norway has changed during the past few years, from about 100% hydropower to a 5-10% import of electricity. It is assumed that the share of imported electric-

ity will be about 10% in the future (Ruud et al. 1998). Elec- tricity or natural gas was used for thermal energy produc- tion at the dairies. Table 1 shows the various steps of the life cycle of milk and its data sources.

The consumption of energy and water resources was con- sidered. The environmental parameters examined were glo- bal warming potential, acidification, eutrophication, photo- oxidant formation, depletion of stratospheric ozone and ecotoxicity. The amount of pesticides used was listed and the amount of antibiotics used was accounted for.

milk and meat production; this allocation method was used to apportion meat and milk in this study. All of the fodder needed for lactation, 50% of the fodder for maintenance and 50% of that, for the recruiting of cows' calves was cred- ited to the milk. This means that 65% of the total amount of the fodder units for the milk cow was assigned to milk. The fodder needed for the growth of a foetus, for calves designated for slaughter and 50% of the fodder for mainte- nance, a total of 35%, was credited to the meat production. In Table 2, the percentage of allocation to milk, all the fod- der, and the fertilisers produced for combined milk and meat production were included, as well as the fodder and fertilisers needed for calves and COWS to be slaughtered and bulls, which are the reasons for the changing percentages. The calcula- tions have taken into account the recruiting of milk cows, average duration of life, and the distribution of sex.

An allocation of manure is normally not necessary in dairy farming. Since the manure stays at the farm and it is used as a fertiliser, it is not looked upon as a product (Cederberg and Mattsson 2000). The skins of the COWS are normally
also omitted. Both in terms of mass and economical value, it is negligible compared to milk and meat.

Allocation in dairy processing. Normally a dairy produces a varied mix of products (cultured milk, cream, yoghurt, cheese). The use of resources and the environmental loads from the dairy have to be allocated among the different prod- ucts. Allocation was performed by the permitted emissions of organic matter given by the Norwegian Environmental Protection Agency (SFT) (Hogaas Eide and Ohlsson 1998); this is directly related to the emissions of organic matter. Larger permitted emissions of BOD7 (Biological Oxygen Demand) indicate a higher degree of processing, which also indicates a greater demand for energy because of more pro- cessing. It was concluded in an earlier study that this method is feasible for the allocation of emissions to water and air from a dairy, as well as for the allocation of energy con- sumption of the various dairy products (Hogaas Eide 1998). The BOD7 emissions permitted in Norway are 1 kg per tonne of fresh drinking milk, 2 kg per tonne of cultured milk, 7 kg per tonne of yoghurt and 8.5 kg per tonne of butter.

Allocation in distribution. The drinking milk is distributed to the retailers, along with other dairy products and, in some places, also eggs, meat and vegetables. The measured share of drinking milk on the truck, based on mass, was used to allocate between the milk and the other products. In Table 2, the allocation of the different life cycle steps and subsystems is shown.

20: Results of resource consumption of energy and water are shown and the impact categories taken into account are the ecological effects of global warming (GWP), depletion of the stratospheric ozone (ODP), eutrophication, acidification, ecotoxicity and photo-oxidant formation (POCP). The amounts of pesticides and antibiotics used are listed. Fig. 2 shows the energy consumption for the life cycle of milk.

Except for the dairy processing at the small dairy, the agri- cultural step contributed most to the total consumption of energy. The fodder cultivated at the farm (pasture, silage and hay) represented 90% of the total needed and consumed 35% of the total energy of the agricultural step, which was derived from fossil fuels. The fertiliser industry was also a large energy consumer, 31%. The electricity accounted for in the milk farming, to chill the milk and to operate the milking equipment, with the pumping to the tank lorry in- cluded, represented 13% of the energy of the agriculture.

For the small dairy, the internal processing was an obvious hot spot. Also the packaging, including its transport to dairy, and the retailer and consumer steps including transport from retailer to home, were hot spots. However, most of the en- ergy used in the packaging step was renewable. The trans- port from retailer to the homes made up 24% of the energy used in the retailer and consumer phase. The dairy trans- port steps, transport to dairy and distribution, had an influ-

ence, although not as much as expected, owing to the fact that the distances of transport are generally very long in Nor- way, especially in the area of the small dairy. The total energy consumption per litre of milk throughout the life cycle, for the large, middle-sized and small dairies was 3.6 MJ, 3.9 MJ and 6.3 MJ, respectively. For waste management, the energy fig- ures were negative, -16 MJ, -2 MJ and -5 MJ, respectively, because energy production from incineration was credited to the system. The energy consumed by the transport for the three dairies differed widely. The small dairy has to transport the milk very long distances, both to the dairy and to the retailers. At the small dairy, 0.8 MJ was consumed to transport 1 litre of milk, which was approximately twice as much as for the large and middle-sized dairies.

Fig. 3 shows the maximum potential eutrophication for milk production. Milk farming was the greatest contributor to the eutrophication potential in the agricultural step; it is shown on a 1/10 scale in the diagram. The leakage of nitro- gen and phosphorus, and the ammonia losses from manure, calculated as a worst case, made up 92% of the eutrophica- tion potential of milk farming. Milk emitted to wastewater (milk not delivered by the farm because of medication as well as water mixed-milk from cleaning of equipment) made up the rest. The emissions of COD, 3% of which contrib- uted to eutrophication, almost all originate from milk leav- ing the system, and 80% of these COD emissions came from the milk disposed of by the farm due to medication of the cattle. Milk not delivered because of medication is not al- lowed to be used as fodder for calves.

The eutrophication potential of the dairy processing together with the retailer and consumer steps was caused by the loss of milk and, in the dairy, also by emissions of nitrogen from the cleaning agents. The loss of milk at the consumer stage was important even when this loss was only 0.97% (10 ml per
Fig. 3: Maximum eutrophication potential of milk (agriculture shown on a 1/10 scale)

litre of milk). The dairy processing at the middle-sized dairy contributed the least to eutrophication. The maximum poten- tial of acidification of the life cycle of milk is shown in Fig. 4. The agriculture was clearly the greatest contributor to the potential acidification. About 90% of the acidification poten- tial of agriculture was due to ammonia losses from manure.

The depletion of stratospheric ozone by milk production was to a great extent a result of emissions from the cooling media in the tanks at the farm, the cold storage at the dairy, the cooling in the distribution lorries and the refrigeration

in households. The emissions at dairies were calculated from the yearly filling of cooling media. The cooling medium mainly used at farms, dairies and in distribution lorries is HCFC-22 (hydrochlorofluorocarbons). There is work in progress to replace the CFCs and HCFCs by natural cooling media and HFCs (Solgaard 2000). Emissions of cooling media from retailers were also accounted for and allocated to milk. In tanks and other cooling equipment at the dairy, ammonia was used as the cooling media. The ozone deple- tion potential (ODP) (given as CFC-11 equivalents or ODPs) was 0.30, 0.36 and 0.26 for the small, middle-sized and large
Fig. 4: The maximum potential of acidification

dairies, respectively. For all of the dairies, 0.25 ODP per functional unit was from the cooling tanks at the farms and 0.01 ODP from the distribution lorries. The annual supply of cooling media needed at the dairies varied. In Fig. 5, the ozone depletion potential (ODP) is shown, divided on the different sources for emissions of cooling media.

The global warming potential in Fig. 6, assessed for direct greenhouse gases (CO, 2) CH4, N2O and HCFC-22) was cal- culated for a time perspective of 100 years. The agriculture contributed the most to the global warming potential. The

differences in global warming potential for the seven steps of the three dairies was a result of the variation in energy consumption. This does not include waste management, for which the differences are due to the amount and type of waste; another factor here that influences global warming is whether waste is landfilled or incinerated.

In Table 3, the distribution of the greenhouse gases contribut- ing to global warming for the middle-sized dairy is shown. The main contributor to global warming is shown for each life cycle step by shading. The main contributor of the agricul-
Fig. 6: Global warming potential, kg CO2 equivalents per FU in a100-year time perspective
Table 3: Distribution of the contribution to global warming potential of four greenhouse gases at the middle-sized dairy

ture to global warming was CH4, which is emitted mostly from the cattle. The other important contributors were CO2, generated mainly by field operations and transport, but also by production of fertilisers and N2O, which are fertiliser-in- duced emissions. It was assumed that 14% of the milk pack- aging and 35% of other waste from the dairy industry were landfilled. The contribution to global warming potential from waste management was principally the methane emissions from landfilling, but there were also emissions from incineration and transport of waste. For the dairy, it was found that the emissions of cooling media were rather significant.

The potential photo-oxidant formation is shown in Fig. 7. The main contributor to this impact category is CO when the 'maximal difference' scenario is used (Finnveden et al. 1992). The highest degree of photo-oxidant formation us- ing this scenario was found in the step of retailer and con- sumer. The most important source of CO was the petrol consumed by the consumer's car when shopping. The fod-
der production in the agricultural step was also an impor- tant source of CO.

The ecotoxicity was assessed based by the CML (Centre of Environmental Science, Leiden University) provisional method and the findings are given in Fig. 8. It was found that the waste management was the principal contributor to ecotoxicity. The emissions of heavy metals, especially from landfilling, were of the greatest importance. Agriculture and the transport steps were also of a substantial consequence. The extraction of fossil fuels, which were used for transport and field operations, was the main contributor to the emis- sions contributing to ecotoxicity, caused by waterborne emissions of oil and heavy metals. The pesticides were not assessed by this method. Koudijs and Dutilh (1998) presented aquatic ecotoxicity equivalency factors for 65 pesticides. However, all the pesticides used in this study were not in- cluded. The amount of antibiotics used was 1.5 g per func- tional unit; there was 15.5 kg of milk that was not delivered by the farm because of medication.

Table 4 shows the consumption of active ingredients of pesti- cides per functional unit at the small dairy, divided among the fodder components. The values for the small dairy were gen- erally somewhat larger than those for the other dairies, since more milk was needed to bring 1,000 litres to the consumer when the loss at the small dairy was higher. None of the pesticides used were found to be highly toxic. However, the use of glyphosate was found to be a hot spot. The amounts of glyphosate, used in cultivation of hay and silage, was larger than for the other pesticides employed, which contributed to higher leakage. The other crops (grain, sugar beets and rape seed) were used in the concentrate feed, which made up only 10% of the total amount of fodder. Glyphosate is classified in risk class B, which means it is harmful, but it is not found to be very toxic to humans.

Water was consumed primarily by the cleaning processes at both the farm and the dairy. The amount of water consumed by the cattle was not included. At the farm, the water used was found to be 0.3 1 per litre of milk. At the dairies, the amount of water needed varied. The small, middle-sized and the large dairies consumed 1.3 litres, 1.05 litres and 1.2 litres per litre drinking milk, respectively.

Except for the dairy processing at the small dairy, the agri- cultural step contributed most to the total consumption of energy. Also the packaging, including its transport to dairy, and the retailer and consumer steps including transport from retailer to home, were hot spots. However, most of the en- ergy used in the packaging step was renewable. The trans- port from retailer to the homes made up 24% of the energy used in the retailer and consumer phase. The dairy trans- port steps, transport to dairy and distribution, had an influ-

ence, although not as much as expected, owing to the fact that the distances of transport are generally very long in Nor- way, especially in the area of the small dairy. At the small dairy, 0.8 MJ was consumed to transport 1 litre of milk, which was approximately twice as much as for the large and middle-sized dairies.

The loss of milk at the consumer stage was important even when this loss was only 0.97% (10 ml per
Fig. 3: Maximum eutrophication potential of milk (agriculture shown on a 1/10 scale)

litre of milk). The dairy processing at the middle-sized dairy contributed the least to eutrophication. The maximum poten- tial of acidification of the life cycle of milk is shown in Fig. 4. The depletion of stratospheric ozone by milk production was to a great extent a result of emissions from the cooling media in the tanks at the farm, the cold storage at the dairy, the cooling in the distribution lorries and the refrigeration

in households. The emissions at dairies were calculated from the yearly filling of cooling media. dairies, respectively. For all of the dairies, 0.25 ODP per functional unit was from the cooling tanks at the farms and 0.01 ODP from the distribution lorries. Fig. 3 shows the maximum potential eutrophication for milk production. Milk farming was the greatest contributor to the eutrophication potential in the agricultural step; it is shown on a 1/10 scale in the diagram. The leakage of nitro- gen and phosphorus, and the ammonia losses from manure, calculated as a worst case, made up 92% of the eutrophica- tion potential of milk farming. Milk emitted to wastewater (milk not delivered by the farm because of medication as well as water mixed-milk from cleaning of equipment) made up the rest. The emissions of COD, 3% of which contrib- uted to eutrophication, almost all originate from milk leav- ing the system, and 80% of these COD emissions came from the milk disposed of by the farm due to medication of the cattle. Milk not delivered because of medication is not al- lowed to be used as fodder for calves.

The eutrophication potential of the dairy processing together with the retailer and consumer steps was caused by the loss of milk and, in the dairy, also by emissions of nitrogen from the cleaning agents. The loss of milk at the consumer stage was important even when this loss was only 0.97% (10 ml per
Fig. 3: Maximum eutrophication potential of milk (agriculture shown on a 1/10 scale)

litre of milk). The dairy processing at the middle-sized dairy contributed the least to eutrophication. The maximum poten- tial of acidification of the life cycle of milk is shown in Fig. 4. The agriculture was clearly the greatest contributor to the potential acidification. About 90% of the acidification poten- tial of agriculture was due to ammonia losses from manure.

The depletion of stratospheric ozone by milk production was to a great extent a result of emissions from the cooling media in the tanks at the farm, the cold storage at the dairy, the cooling in the distribution lorries and the refrigeration

in households. The emissions at dairies were calculated from the yearly filling of cooling media. The cooling medium mainly used at farms, dairies and in distribution lorries is HCFC-22 (hydrochlorofluorocarbons). There is work in progress to replace the CFCs and HCFCs by natural cooling media and HFCs (Solgaard 2000). Emissions of cooling media from retailers were also accounted for and allocated to milk. In tanks and other cooling equipment at the dairy, ammonia was used as the cooling media. The ozone deple- tion potential (ODP) (given as CFC-11 equivalents or ODPs) was 0.30, 0.36 and 0.26 for the small, middle-sized and large
Fig. 4: The maximum potential of acidification

dairies, respectively. For all of the dairies, 0.25 ODP per functional unit was from the cooling tanks at the farms and 0.01 ODP from the distribution lorries. The annual supply of cooling media needed at the dairies varied. In Fig. 5, the ozone depletion potential (ODP) is shown, divided on the different sources for emissions of cooling media.

The global warming potential in Fig. 6, assessed for direct greenhouse gases (CO, 2) CH4, N2O and HCFC-22) was cal- culated for a time perspective of 100 years. The agriculture contributed the most to the global warming potential. The

differences in global warming potential for the seven steps of the three dairies was a result of the variation in energy consumption. This does not include waste management, for which the differences are due to the amount and type of waste; another factor here that influences global warming is whether waste is landfilled or incinerated.

In Table 3, the distribution of the greenhouse gases contribut- ing to global warming for the middle-sized dairy is shown. The main contributor to global warming is shown for each life cycle step by shading. The main contributor of the agricul-
Fig. 6: Global warming potential, kg CO2 equivalents per FU in a100-year time perspective
Table 3: Distribution of the contribution to global warming potential of four greenhouse gases at the middle-sized dairy

ture to global warming was CH4, which is emitted mostly from the cattle. The other important contributors were CO2, generated mainly by field operations and transport, but also by production of fertilisers and N2O, which are fertiliser-in- duced emissions. It was assumed that 14% of the milk pack- aging and 35% of other waste from the dairy industry were landfilled. The contribution to global warming potential from waste management was principally the methane emissions from landfilling, but there were also emissions from incineration and transport of waste. For the dairy, it was found that the emissions of cooling media were rather significant.

The potential photo-oxidant formation is shown in Fig. 7. The main contributor to this impact category is CO when the 'maximal difference' scenario is used (Finnveden et al. 1992). The highest degree of photo-oxidant formation us- ing this scenario was found in the step of retailer and con- sumer. The most important source of CO was the petrol consumed by the consumer's car when shopping. The fod-
der production in the agricultural step was also an impor- tant source of CO.

The ecotoxicity was assessed based by the CML (Centre of Environmental Science, Leiden University) provisional method and the findings are given in Fig. 8. It was found that the waste management was the principal contributor to ecotoxicity. The emissions of heavy metals, especially from landfilling, were of the greatest importance. Agriculture and the transport steps were also of a substantial consequence. The extraction of fossil fuels, which were used for transport and field operations, was the main contributor to the emis- sions contributing to ecotoxicity, caused by waterborne emissions of oil and heavy metals. The pesticides were not assessed by this method. Koudijs and Dutilh (1998) presented aquatic ecotoxicity equivalency factors for 65 pesticides. However, all the pesticides used in this study were not in- cluded. The amount of antibiotics used was 1.5 g per func- tional unit; there was 15.5 kg of milk that was not delivered by the farm because of medication.

Table 4 shows the consumption of active ingredients of pesti- cides per functional unit at the small dairy, divided among the fodder components. The values for the small dairy were gen- erally somewhat larger than those for the other dairies, since more milk was needed to bring 1,000 litres to the consumer when the loss at the small dairy was higher. None of the pesticides used were found to be highly toxic. However, the use of glyphosate was found to be a hot spot. The amounts of glyphosate, used in cultivation of hay and silage, was larger than for the other pesticides employed, which contributed to higher leakage. The other crops (grain, sugar beets and rape seed) were used in the concentrate feed, which made up only 10% of the total amount of fodder. Glyphosate is classified in risk class B, which means it is harmful, but it is not found to be very toxic to humans.

Fig. 8: Ecotoxicity of the life cycle of milk based on the CML provisional method

Water was consumed primarily by the cleaning processes at both the farm and the dairy. The amount of water consumed by the cattle was not included. At the farm, the water used was found to be 0.3 1 per litre of milk. At the dairies, the amount of water needed varied. The small, middle-sized and the large dairies consumed 1.3 litres, 1.05 litres and 1.2 litres per litre drinking milk, respectively.

Table 4 shows the consumption of active ingredients of pesti- cides per functional unit at the small dairy, divided among the fodder components. The values for the small dairy were gen- erally somewhat larger than those for the other dairies, since more milk was needed to bring 1,000 litres to the consumer when the loss at the small dairy was higher. ence, although not as much as expected, owing to the fact that the distances of transport are generally very long in Nor- way, especially in the area of the small dairy. 32: <header>3.1 The hot spots of the life cycle of milk </header>
The agricultural production was found to be of significance for all of the environmental parameters. For eutrophication

and acidification, the agriculture was vital owing to the ammonia emissions and the leakage of nutrients from ma- nure. It should be noted that 'worst case' assumptions were used, which means the total potential effects of each sub- stance were calculated. The contribution to acidification from fossil fuel emissions was 13% in the agricultural step; this was very small compared with the acidifying effect of the ammonia emissions. The fact that electricity and natural gas were used as a source of thermal energy reduces the acidifi- cation potential of the dairies.
The packaging was found to influence the result for several of the environmental parameters considered. Including its transport to the dairy, it was found to consume more energy
Table 4: Amount of active ingredients and qualitative assessment of human health of pesticides used for fodder production

Fodder component|Type and percentage of active ingredient|Amount of active ingredient, g/1000 milk|Human health risk class|R-phrases Silage and hay|Glyphosate 33%|34.0|B|R36 Irritating eyes Grain|Propikonazol 62,5% Pirimicarb 50% Tribenuronmethyl 53%|0.00367 0.00148 0.000311|C B C|R36 Irritating eyes R36/38 Irritating eyes and skin Sugar beets|Metamitron 70% Phenmediphfam 16% Ethofumesate 11.5% Chloridazon 65%|0.0564 0.00218 0.00156 0.0179|C C B C|R38 Irritating skin R36/38 Irritating eyes and skin Rape seed|Metazachlor 33% Esfenvalerate 5%|0.759 0.00233|B B|R36 Irritating eyes

than the combined consumption of milk transport to the dairy and the distribution. However, most of the energy is renewable. The consumer phase also had a major influence on the result for several parameters. Both the storage and the transport from retailer to home were found to be crucial for the energy use. The storage at the retailer and in refrig- erators at homes used four times more energy than the shop- ping by car. The information available on consumer behav- iour was insufficient; the loss of milk by the consumer was measured to be 1%, which may be too low. The measure- ments were based on the amount of product sticking to the empty cartons and glasses. Milk is a fresh product, with a rather short storage time, which may increase loss in the consumer phase. 38: <header>3.2 Size of production and degree of automation </header>

Small-scale production is often assumed to be more environ- mentally friendly than large-scale. The study shows that this is not always true. Both the total results and those from the dairies in particular show that the small dairy contributed more to most of the environmental effects than the large and the middle-sized ones per functional unit; the exception is deple- tion of stratosperic ozone, which originates from emissions of cooling media. For energy consumption, the milk processing at the small dairy was an obvious hot spot. This indicates that smaller volumes of production consume more energy per unit, which is to be expected, since the equipment has to be cleaned and started up regardless of the volume. On the other hand, the small dairy had a more complex mix of products (drink- ing milk, cultured milk and butter). The allocation itself may be a source of error. The production of butter may have been more energy consuming than the allocation factor takes into account, because the dairy produced very small volumes at a time and starting up and stopping the processing demand a large amount of energy. This could mean that too small a share of the total energy consumption was allocated to the butter and, consequently, too much was charged to the milk. The large dairy turned out to be the best for all impact categories, except for eutrophication, for which the middle-sized dairy had the lowest impact.

The differences between the results for the dairies are larger in the total results because of the greater environmental ef- fects from the transports needed by the small dairy. In addi- tion, the location of the small dairy gave rise to long trans- port distances for packaging, additives and wasted packag- ing. In contrast to these results, statistics (Solgaard 2000) has shown that smaller and less automated dairies some- times consume less energy, and also emit less per litre of milk than larger dairies. Although it may be difficult to keep track of the actual production activity in a large automated dairy; it is hard to generalize because of the great individual differences and routines at the dairies.

The extent of automation of the milk processing at the three dairies also varied. The large dairy was the most highly au- tomated one, and the middle-sized one the least. As to eutro- phication, the middle-sized dairy, which also had the least automation, contributed less than the others; the loss of milk and the emissions of cleaning agents were also smaller than at the other dairies, as well as the water consumption. Less automation give the operators the possibility to control the draining of milk from tanks and pipelines better than at more

automated dairies. Eutrophication is mainly caused by loss of milk not drained from the equipment. In a highly automat- ed dairy, as in a large dairy, the operators may sometimes lose touch with parts of the actual process (Homleid 1999). These results show that the management in a dairy, or in the industry in general, influences the environmental perfor- mance in particular.

Three dairies may be too few for general conclusions to be drawn about the influence of the size and the degree of au- tomation. The individual variations depend on external fac- tors such as the industry culture, the employers, and the supervision of the governmental legislation in the area, as well as the public opinion. However, this study has shown that small dairies have greater environmental impact than larger ones. Automation decreases the energy use, but in- creases the losses of milk. A larger number (10-15) of the 70 Norwegian dairies could have been studied to facilitate even more conclusive results.
39: <header>3.2 Size of production and degree of automation </header>

Small-scale production is often assumed to be more environ- mentally friendly than large-scale. The study shows that this is not always true. Both the total results and those from the dairies in particular show that the small dairy contributed more to most of the environmental effects than the large and the middle-sized ones per functional unit; the exception is deple- tion of stratosperic ozone, which originates from emissions of cooling media. For energy consumption, the milk processing at the small dairy was an obvious hot spot. This indicates that smaller volumes of production consume more energy per unit, which is to be expected, since the equipment has to be cleaned and started up regardless of the volume. On the other hand, the small dairy had a more complex mix of products (drink- ing milk, cultured milk and butter). The allocation itself may be a source of error. The production of butter may have been more energy consuming than the allocation factor takes into account, because the dairy produced very small volumes at a time and starting up and stopping the processing demand a large amount of energy. This could mean that too small a share of the total energy consumption was allocated to the butter and, consequently, too much was charged to the milk. The large dairy turned out to be the best for all impact categories, except for eutrophication, for which the middle-sized dairy had the lowest impact.

The differences between the results for the dairies are larger in the total results because of the greater environmental ef- fects from the transports needed by the small dairy. In addi- tion, the location of the small dairy gave rise to long trans- port distances for packaging, additives and wasted packag- ing. In contrast to these results, statistics (Solgaard 2000) has shown that smaller and less automated dairies some- times consume less energy, and also emit less per litre of milk than larger dairies. Although it may be difficult to keep track of the actual production activity in a large automated dairy; it is hard to generalize because of the great individual differences and routines at the dairies.

The extent of automation of the milk processing at the three dairies also varied. The large dairy was the most highly au- tomated one, and the middle-sized one the least. As to eutro- phication, the middle-sized dairy, which also had the least automation, contributed less than the others; the loss of milk and the emissions of cleaning agents were also smaller than at the other dairies, as well as the water consumption. Less automation give the operators the possibility to control the draining of milk from tanks and pipelines better than at more

automated dairies. Eutrophication is mainly caused by loss of milk not drained from the equipment. In a highly automat- ed dairy, as in a large dairy, the operators may sometimes lose touch with parts of the actual process (Homleid 1999). These results show that the management in a dairy, or in the industry in general, influences the environmental perfor- mance in particular.

Three dairies may be too few for general conclusions to be drawn about the influence of the size and the degree of au- tomation. The individual variations depend on external fac- tors such as the industry culture, the employers, and the supervision of the governmental legislation in the area, as well as the public opinion. However, this study has shown that small dairies have greater environmental impact than larger ones. Automation decreases the energy use, but in- creases the losses of milk. A larger number (10-15) of the 70 Norwegian dairies could have been studied to facilitate even more conclusive results.

42: The distances of transport did not influence the result as much as was expected, except for photo-oxidant formation and ecotoxicity. At the small dairy especially, and also in Norway in general, the distances of transport are rather long. However, transportation of the milk, from the farm to the dairy, as well as from the dairy to the retailers, did not seem to be of vital importance for the results.
43: Possible environmental effects of antibiotics in wastewater were not included. Since there were no weighting factors, it was not possible to use LCA methodology to judge whether there are impacts from antibiotics on meat, milk, or the en- vironment. The most common antibiotic used is benzylpeni- cillin, which breaks down easily and does not have any nega- tive long term effects in the nature (Torud et al. 1996).

This study shows that agricultural production was the ma- jor hot spot of the system. Based on the calculations and assumptions made, it can be concluded that the two larger dairies influenced the environment less than the smallest dairy. The transport influenced the results relatively little.
44: There are several ways to reduce the environmental impacts of milk. Nine alternatives are offered:

<list>To lower the potential of both acidification and eutrophi- cation, the prevention of the ammonia emissions and leakage of nutrients from the agricultural production is very important. 
The eutrophication potential of milk farming can also be reduced by finding alternative ways to handle the milk not delivered by the farm due to the use of antibiotics. Reducing the need for antibiotics by improving the ani- mal health would lower the amount of lost milk.</list>

<list>The photo-oxidant formation can be curtailed to a large extent by reducing the use of cars when shopping. Shop- ping on the Internet, which may have positive effects on the distribution and shopping patterns, or driving elec- tric cars are foreseeable future improvements. 
There is work in progress to replace the cooling media by natural cooling media, such as NH3, or less environ- mentally harmful cooling media, such as HFC. 
Organic farming has been found to offer better results from an environmental point of view (Cederberg and Mattsson 2000); it can be assumed that an increased share of organic farming in Norway would reduce the overall environmental impacts of milk production. 
To curtail the global warming potential and ecotoxicity, the amount of landfilled packaging and other waste products leaving the system could be recycled to a greater extent or incineration could be combined with energy recycling. 
The energy from incineration of waste should replace that from fossil fuels rather than Norwegian electricity, which does not influence the environmental effects included. 
Optimisation of the size and of the automation in relation to the transport distances may reduce the overall impacts of the dairy industry, although more investigation may be necessary before drawing definite conclusions. 
With regard to loss of milk and energy consumption, dairy processing can be improved, e.g. by increased en- ergy recovery in the dairy. 
The management and implementation of environmental thinking in a dairy could also improve the environmen- tal performance.</list>
